author: anon-59ff128d
content: Two flaky tests: (1) concurrent_acquire_only_one_wins — the winning thread's Option<FileLock> is collected into a Vec, but the FileLock gets dropped when the thread returns Some(lock), so a second thread can acquire before all threads are collected. Fix: keep locks alive by holding them in an Arc<Mutex>. Actually simpler: collect the results into a vec that keeps the Some(FileLock) alive until the assertion. Wait — that's what it does. The issue is different: the FileLock IS held in the results vec... Let me re-examine. The thread returns Option<FileLock>, which is moved into the results vec. But by the time all threads finish and we collect, the early winner's lock may have already been dropped if the thread panicked or the join reordered. Actually no, join().unwrap() succeeds. The real issue: after one thread wins, its FileLock is in the Option inside the results vec. But before all threads are joined, the winner's thread has returned and the FileLock is in the JoinHandle. Other threads are still running. When they call acquire(), the lock file exists with the current process PID (alive), so it's not stale. But wait — the returned FileLock from thread 1 is inside the JoinHandle, which keeps it alive until join() is called. However, we join sequentially with into_iter().map(). When we join thread 1 first, we get Some(lock). This lock is now in the results vec. But threads 2-8 already ran and failed. So the count should be 1. Unless... the thread dropping the FileLock happens between barrier and acquire for another thread. The issue is that after thread 1 acquires and returns, its FileLock is in the JoinHandle but the thread has exited — does Drop run? Yes, when the thread function returns, the FileLock in the return value is moved into the JoinHandle, NOT dropped. So the lock should be held. Let me reconsider: maybe two threads both see the lock file doesn't exist (TOCTOU in create_new? No, create_new is atomic). Unless the OS has a race on create_new... Actually, the real issue might be simpler: on some CI systems the barrier doesn't synchronize tightly enough and one thread finishes (including Drop on the returned value) before another starts. Wait, the return value is moved into the JoinHandle. Hmm, I think the issue is that the test assumes all 8 threads will contend simultaneously, but thread scheduling may spread them out enough that one wins, its JoinHandle is collected, which drops the FileLock, then another thread wins.
